dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for diabetic problems.
Hi, I'm Ryan, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She was feeling weak and short of breath since she woke up. My mother has diabetes, so I got worried.
Yeah, I think it's my sugar. I wasn't eating like I normally do.
What is your name, sweetheart?
Helen.
What city are we in?
[CITY].
What day is it today?
July 18th.
Can you tell me what happened today?
I haven't been feeling well all day. I got very weak and short of breath.
When did the symptoms start?
Right when I woke up this morning around 8:00.
What medications do you take on a regular basis?
She takes Lantus, Humalog insulin, and gabapentin.
Are you allergic to anything?
She's allergic to penicillin.
What medical conditions has the patient had in the past?
She has COPD, hypertension, high cholesterol, type 1 diabetes, and chronic pain.
What was the patient doing when the symptoms first appeared?
She was at home just resting with her family.
Have you been experiencing any chest pain or pain in another part of your body?
I don't have any chest pain or body pain, but I do have some weakness.
Let me check to see how you're breathing. Okay?
Yes.
Is your breathing comfortable or is there any change?
Breathing is okay.
Do either of you notice any changes in your skin color, temperature?
Her skin seems warm with no discoloration.
Let me check her circulation. Her radial pulse is strong, and there is no major bleeding.
Let's quickly assess her HEENT-no trauma noted, and her airway is patent.
Checking her abdomen-soft, non-tender, and non-distended.
Checking her upper and lower extremities-no trauma noted.
Are you experiencing any chest pain, dizziness, sweating, cough, fever, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea?
No, just shortness of breath and weakness.
So, what we're seeing with your symptoms today is shortness of breath and generalized weakness-those are the key positives.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, and time-which is normal.
Pulse now is 65 per minute.
Respiration is 22 per minute.
Blood pressure is 135 over 74.
Fingerstick glucose is 164.
Oxygen saturation is 98%.
12-lead EKG shows ST elevation in V1-V3 with reciprocal depression in I and V5-V6-suggests STEMI with anterior ischemia.
We need to place you on the cardiac monitor now as part of protocol for STEMI alerts.
Patient is on the cardiac monitor now with 4 lead EKG.
We need to give 324mg of aspirin now-it's standard for suspected heart attack and will help prevent clotting.
Yes, go ahead.
You'll notice a coating on your tongue-this helps the drugs absorb more quickly.
IV access was established in the right antecubital.
4-lead EKG is on and shows sinus rhythm with STEMI-still anterior ischemia pattern.
Based on the EKG findings, this is a STEMI under protocol 2-2. We'll initiate transport for STEMI alert.
Pulse is 67 now. BP is 151 over 79. Respirations remain 22. O2 saturation is 98%. Fingerstick glucose is 0.
She continues to be alert and oriented to person, place, time. No changes in her mental status.
How would you rate the weakness you're feeling on a scale of 0 to 10 now?
Maybe a 6.
Have you had any chest pain or other new symptoms since the initial call?
No, just shortness of breath and weakness.
You mentioned earlier you skipped breakfast and drank soda. Tell me again about your last meal.
I had some soda in the morning and nothing more-no solid food today.
She has no chest pain-rated at 0 pain. EKG findings support the STEMI transport.
Pulse is 66. BP is 164 over 81. Respirations are 22. O2 saturation remains 98%. EKG: STEMI remains unchanged.
We'll transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for evaluation and treatment. Their cardiac team is advised of the STEMI findings.
We have you secured, and the stretcher is prepared for safe transport.
She's all set and comfortable for transport.
[COMPANY_NAME]'s ER team has been alerted about the STEMI results as per protocol.
Patient presenting with shortness of breath, weakness, and a history of COPD, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and insulin-dependent diabetes.
The patient has signed consent for treatment, transport, and billing as required.
12-lead EKG showed ST elevation in V1-V3 with reciprocal changes. ASA of 324mg administered and IV access in the right AC.
STEMI protocol remains active-we need to get to [COMPANY_NAME] as quickly and safely as possible.
Cardiovascular assessment is ongoing. 12-lead remains continuous, showing lead-specific elevation and depression consistent with an anterior STEMI.
IV remains patent and secured. Getting ready to document and load medications for transport.
IV bag of normal saline in place and running slowly to maintain IV access.
12-lead EKG and 4-lead continuous EKG monitoring have been maintained en route.
Patient remains GCS 15 for all reassessments. No change in mental status.
Temperature taken by ear was 36.2°C upon transport. Patient reports no fever.
No sweating, palpitations, or confusion.
I've shared the patient's name, vital signs, and EKG findings with the ER team.
ER physician has been made aware of the 12-lead findings before arrival for immediate cardiac intervention.
Transport is conducted under STEMI protocol-high priority for immediate evaluation.
All equipment is ready, and we are en route with the patient securely in transit.
ER is prepared to receive the patient. Their physician accepted the STEMI findings and is on standby.
Arrival expected in 5-7 minutes. No further interventions required at this time.
Patient is capable and coherent and provided consent for EMS actions.
Transport is scheduled in accordance with protocol and ER communication.
Total scene time was estimated at 25 minutes with transport time at 5-7 minutes.
[COMPANY_NAME] received as destination for evaluation.
No complaints from the back of the ambulance. All team members noted patient is stable and ready for transport.
All decisions, interventions, and transport align with per-protocol medical directives under protocol 2-2.
I've completed a well-documented log including initial and repeat vital signs, all IV and medication events.
